dependencies.description: L2 mode uses Ethertype 0x88F7 — requires VLAN reachability. L3 mode uses UDP ports 319 (event) and 320 (general) — requires IP routing.
dependencies.name: Physical Ethernet connectivity (L2) or IP connectivity (L3)

dependencies.description: Sub-microsecond accuracy requires PHY-level hardware timestamps. Software timestamping adds variable delay that degrades accuracy to milliseconds.
dependencies.name: Hardware timestamping capability

dependencies.description: PTP domain number must match on all devices in the timing domain. Mismatched domains cause devices to ignore each other's Announce messages.
dependencies.name: PTP domain match between master and slave

dependencies.description: SyncE requires PHY-level support for recovering clock from the received Ethernet signal. Not available on all interfaces — check platform datasheet.
dependencies.name: SyncE PHY support for recovered clock

failure_modes.description: PTP port stays in LISTENING or UNCALIBRATED state, unable to lock to a master clock. Local clock runs free, causing time drift on downstream clients.

resolutions.action: Verify Announce messages are received: 'show ptp clock' or equivalent — check for master info
Verify Announce messages are received: 'show ptp clock' or equivalent — check for master info

Check PTP domain matches on both ends
Check PTP domain matches on both ends
Verify transport: L2 VLAN reachability or UDP port 319/320 not blocked by ACL
Verify transport: L2 VLAN reachability or UDP port 319/320 not blocked by ACL
Verify transport: L2 VLAN reachability or UDP port 319/320 not blocked by ACL
Check BMC priority settings: lower priority1 = better — master should have lower value than slave
Check BMC priority settings: lower priority1 = better — master should have lower value than slave
Check BMC priority settings: lower priority1 = better — master should have lower value than slave
Verify one-step vs two-step mode consistency
Verify one-step vs two-step mode consistency
Verify one-step vs two-step mode consistency

root_causes.cause: No Announce messages reaching slave (VLAN/ACL/firewall blocking Ethertype 0x88F7 or UDP 319/320)
BMC priority misconfigured — slave has better clock attributes than intended master (lower priority1 = better)
One-step/two-step mode mismatch between master and slave
PTP domain mismatch between master and slave

resolutions.action: Verify ESMC is enabled on all SyncE interfaces
Verify ESMC is enabled on all SyncE interfaces
Verify ESMC is enabled on all SyncE interfaces

SyncE not configured on intermediate nodes in the path

failure_modes.description: PTP is locked to a master but the time offset or PDV (Packet Delay Variation) is too high for the application. Telecom applications require sub-microsecond accuracy.

resolutions.action: Use PTP-aware (boundary clock or transparent clock) switches on the entire path — no non-PTP hops
Use PTP-aware (boundary clock or transparent clock) switches on the entire path — no non-PTP hops
Use PTP-aware (boundary clock or transparent clock) switches on the entire path — no non-PTP hops
Enable hardware timestamping on all PTP nodes
Enable hardware timestamping on all PTP nodes
Enable hardware timestamping on all PTP nodes
Configure one-step mode where hardware supports it (eliminates Follow_Up jitter)
Configure one-step mode where hardware supports it (eliminates Follow_Up jitter)
Configure one-step mode where hardware supports it (eliminates Follow_Up jitter)
Ensure PTP packets get highest QoS priority (DSCP CS7 or queue priority)
Ensure PTP packets get highest QoS priority (DSCP CS7 or queue priority)
Ensure PTP packets get highest QoS priority (DSCP CS7 or queue priority)
Use G.8275.1 profile for telecom — requires full on-path PTP support
Use G.8275.1 profile for telecom — requires full on-path PTP support
Use G.8275.1 profile for telecom — requires full on-path PTP support

root_causes.cause: Non-PTP-aware switches in path adding variable delay (PDV) — breaks end-to-end timing budget
Software timestamping instead of hardware timestamping
Congestion causing packet delay on PTP path — PTP packets not getting highest QoS priority
One-step vs two-step mode mismatch causing timestamp inaccuracy

failure_modes.description: SyncE provides stable frequency but PTP phase/time offset is increasing. Common in hybrid deployments where the PTP path has non-PTP-aware hops.

resolutions.action: Ensure PTP boundary clock mode on all intermediate nodes — no non-PTP hops
Ensure PTP boundary clock mode on all intermediate nodes — no non-PTP hops
Ensure PTP boundary clock mode on all intermediate nodes — no non-PTP hops

PTP boundary clock not configured on intermediate nodes between GM and slave
PTP and SyncE using different reference sources — should both trace to the same grandmaster

failure_modes.description: When the primary grandmaster fails, the BMC algorithm selects the wrong clock as the new grandmaster, causing a time jump or degraded accuracy.

resolutions.action: Configure priority1/priority2 to enforce correct GM hierarchy (lower = better): primary GM priority1=128, backup GM priority1=129
Configure priority1/priority2 to enforce correct GM hierarchy (lower = better): primary GM priority1=128, backup GM priority1=129
Configure priority1/priority2 to enforce correct GM hierarchy (lower = better): primary GM priority1=128, backup GM priority1=129
Set backup GM clock class to appropriate holdover value (Class 7 for locked, Class 52 for holdover) to indicate degraded accuracy during failover
Set backup GM clock class to appropriate holdover value (Class 7 for locked, Class 52 for holdover) to indicate degraded accuracy during failover
Set backup GM clock class to appropriate holdover value (Class 7 for locked, Class 52 for holdover) to indicate degraded accuracy during failover
Tune Announce timeout for faster failover if application requires it (reduce from default 3 to 2)
Tune Announce timeout for faster failover if application requires it (reduce from default 3 to 2)
Tune Announce timeout for faster failover if application requires it (reduce from default 3 to 2)
Deploy redundant GM with GNSS receivers at geographically diverse locations
Deploy redundant GM with GNSS receivers at geographically diverse locations
Deploy redundant GM with GNSS receivers at geographically diverse locations

root_causes.cause: BMC priority misconfigured — priority1/priority2 not set to enforce correct GM hierarchy
Backup GM clock class not set correctly (Class 6 for locked vs Class 7/52 for holdover/freerun)
Announce timeout too long — slow failover leaves slaves in holdover too long
No redundant GM deployed — single point of failure

failure_modes.scenario: Grandmaster failover — BMCA not selecting correct backup
symptoms: On GM failure, wrong clock selected as new grandmaster
Time jump on failover — downstream slaves re-lock with offset
Downstream slaves temporarily free-running during GM re-election

messages.description: Carries clock quality information for the BMC algorithm (grandmaster identity, priority, accuracy, class)
messages.name: Announce
messages.when_sent: Periodically at Announce Interval by master ports

messages.description: Timestamped message from master carrying t1 (departure time) for time transfer to slave
key_fields: Two-Step Flag (if set, precise t1 follows in Follow_Up)
messages.name: Sync
messages.when_sent: Periodically at Sync Interval by master

messages.description: Carries the precise t1 timestamp when operating in two-step mode (hardware timestamp captured after Sync)
messages.name: Follow_Up
messages.when_sent: Immediately after Sync in two-step mode only

messages.description: Sent by slave to master for delay measurement — slave records departure time t3
messages.name: Delay_Req
messages.when_sent: Periodically at Min Delay Request Interval by slave

messages.description: Master responds with t4 (arrival time of Delay_Req) — completes the four-timestamp delay measurement
key_fields: Receive Timestamp (t4 — when Delay_Req arrived at master)
messages.name: Delay_Resp

messages.description: Ethernet Synchronization Messaging Channel — carries Quality Level (QL) for SyncE clock source selection
key_fields: QL-TLV (Quality Level): PRC/PRS (primary reference), SSU-A/SSU-B (secondary), SEC/EEC (equipment clock), DNU (Do Not Use)
messages.name: ESMC (SyncE)

purpose: Network time/frequency synchronization — PTP for phase/time, SyncE for frequency. Critical for mobile backhaul (LTE/5G), carrier Ethernet, and financial trading.

states.description: Port starting up, not yet participating in BMC algorithm or sending messages
states.name: INITIALIZING

states.description: Port listening for Announce messages, participating in BMC (Best Master Clock) algorithm
states.name: LISTENING

states.description: Port selected as best clock source, sending Sync and Announce messages to slaves
states.name: MASTER

states.description: Port locked to a master clock, receiving Sync messages and adjusting local clock
states.name: SLAVE

states.description: Port sees a better path to grandmaster, standing by (not acting as master or slave)
states.name: PASSIVE

states.description: Port has selected a master but the servo loop has not yet locked to it — transitional state
states.name: UNCALIBRATED

states.description: Port has detected a fault condition
states.name: FAULTY

timers.description: Interval between Announce messages carrying clock quality information for the BMC algorithm
timers.name: Announce Interval
timers.tuning_advice: Shorter intervals give faster GM failover but increase control-plane load. G.8275.1 recommends 1s or 2s.

timers.description: Number of missed Announce intervals before declaring master lost
timers.name: Announce Receipt Timeout
timers.on_expiry: Master declared lost, BMC re-election triggered
timers.tuning_advice: Lower values give faster GM failover but risk false triggers on congested networks. Typical: 3.

timers.description: Interval between Sync messages from master to slave for time transfer
timers.name: Sync Interval
timers.tuning_advice: Shorter = better accuracy but more CPU/bandwidth. Mobile backhaul typically 125ms (logSyncInterval=-3). G.8275.1 recommends 62.5ms to 1s.

timers.description: Minimum interval between Delay_Req messages sent by the slave for path delay measurement
timers.name: Min Delay Request Interval
timers.tuning_advice: Should match or be slower than Sync interval. Faster rates improve delay measurement but add load.

timers.description: Ethernet Synchronization Messaging Channel interval for SyncE quality level signaling
timers.name: SyncE ESMC Interval
timers.on_expiry: ESMC PDU sent with current Quality Level (QL) value
timers.tuning_advice: Not tunable — fixed at 1s per standard. ESMC timeout is typically 5s (5 missed ESMCs).

transitions.trigger: BMC determines this port should be master (best clock on this segment)
BMC determines a better master exists on this segment
BMC determines another path to grandmaster is better

transitions.failure_if_stuck: Master clock lost but BMC not promoting this port — check priority and clock class settings
transitions.trigger: Current master fails (Announce receipt timeout), this port becomes master
Better clock source appears (superior Announce received)
Master changes, servo needs to re-lock to new master

transitions.failure_if_stuck: Servo unable to lock — check offset/jitter, hardware timestamping, and path delay variation
transitions.trigger: Servo has locked to new master clock
Hardware fault or PTP clock failure

notes.note: IOS-XE supports PTP (1588v2) and SyncE on ASR 920/903, Catalyst 9000. G.8275.1 telecom profile supported. Hardware timestamping on all supported platforms. 'ptp clock boundary/ordinary' configuration. 'show ptp clock', 'show ptp port'. SyncE with ESMC for frequency distribution.

IOS-XR full PTP + SyncE on ASR 9000, NCS 5500, Cisco 8000. G.8275.1/G.8275.2 profiles. Hardware timestamps with sub-100ns accuracy on NCS platforms. 'ptp clock profile g.8275.1'. 'show ptp platform servo'. SyncE with SSM/ESMC. Critical for mobile backhaul deployments.

NX-OS supports PTP on Nexus 9000 with hardware timestamping. 'feature ptp' required. Supports boundary clock and transparent clock modes. SyncE supported on select Nexus 9300/9500 line cards. 'show ptp clock', 'show ptp port' commands.

PTP/SyncE is a core feature of SAOS — all 5170/5171/8000/WaveRouter platforms include integrated GNSS receivers for grandmaster capability. IEEE 1588v2, G.8275.1, G.8275.2 profiles. Hardware timestamping on all ports. SyncE G.8262/G.8262.1 with ESMC. Hybrid mode (SyncE frequency + PTP phase). ToD (Time of Day) output for legacy equipment. Subject-verb CLI: 'ptp show clock' not 'show ptp clock'. Supports boundary clock, ordinary clock, and grandmaster with GNSS. BITS/T1/E1 timing interfaces on 5000/8000-series.
